intro: Lost Unfound is a production and recording studio built by two people who genuinely love music. We're based in the Forlì / Bologna area of Italy, and we work closely with a small number of artists at a time — because that's the only way we know how to do it well.
There are no layers here. When you work with us, you work with us. We're invested in the song, in the process, and in making sure the record you leave with actually sounds like you.
alessandro_bio: I've been obsessed with recorded music for as long as I can remember — the way a snare sounds in a room, the way a vocal sits in a mix, the way a production can either serve a song or quietly get in its way.

I started studying music production seriously in 2020, through a combination of seminars, books, and a lot of trial and error. I'm currently finishing my third and final year of a sound engineering degree at a professional academy in Bologna, in partnership with Coventry University, which has given me a much more rigorous framework to hang everything I'd already been learning on.

In the studio, I tend to work on the structural and technical side of things: workflow, editing, programming, making sure the pieces of a production are actually doing what they're supposed to do. I'm at home working entirely in the box when the project calls for it, and I enjoy the hybrid approach when there's space for live elements.
What I care about most is making records that feel honest. Not polished for its own sake, not raw for its own sake — just right for the song and the artist behind it.
davide_bio: I came to production through music theory, which I think shapes everything about how I listen. I studied piano and solfeggio for over ten years at Istituto Musicale Masini in Forlì, and later earned my A level certification in classical piano and solfeggio at Conservatorio G. Lettimi in Rimini. All of that time spent understanding harmony, structure, and how musical choices carry emotional weight — it doesn't leave you.

When I work on a song, the first question I'm always asking is whether the arrangement is actually serving the idea at the centre of it. My instinct is to remove before I add, to find what's essential and let that breathe. I help artists develop their ideas, sharpen their arrangements, and make decisions that feel grounded rather than arbitrary, while staying open to the moments when something unexpected turns out to be exactly right.

In the studio I work as assistant engineer, which means I'm the second set of ears and hands in the room during sessions. I want artists to feel comfortable enough to be honest about what they're going for, because that's the only way the work gets interesting.
